zh.Dominican church of Saints Peter and Pau: 愛爾蘭庫迪 高威阿騰利

zh.lifeSummary: 當Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde於1340年出生在愛爾蘭庫迪 高威可藍利卡爾德時，他的父親Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde25歲，母親Grany O'Carroll21歲。他於大約1363年與More O'Madden在愛爾蘭結婚。他們至少是4個兒子的父母。他去世於1387年享年47歲，埋葬在愛爾蘭庫迪 高威阿騰利。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde于1340年出生在爱尔兰庫迪 高威可藍利卡爾德时，他的父亲Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde25岁，母亲Grany O'Carroll21岁。他于大约1363年与More O'Madden在爱尔兰结婚。他们至少是4个儿子的父母。他去世于1387年享年47岁，埋葬在爱尔兰庫迪 高威阿騰利。

nameMeanings.name: Óg
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French/Haitian Alphonse, Gesner, Jacques.
French (Ogé): variant of Ogier or Auger (compare Auge ) and, in North America, also an altered form of the latter. The surname Ogé is also found in Haiti.
Turkish (Öge, also Öğe): ornamental name or nickname from regional öge ‘genius’, or from its archaic homonym meaning ‘fame’.

variants.name: Ogier
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Jean-Jacques.
French and English (of Norman origin): from the Old French personal name Ogier, from ancient Germanic Audagari, composed of the elements audha- ‘wealth, power’ + gaira- ‘lance, spear’.
French: variant of Augier, itself a variant of Auger .

nameMeanings.name: Burke
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Kieran, Siobhan, Aileen, Brennan, Brigid, Donovan, Liam, Murphy, Ulick, Aidan, Dermot.

English and Irish (of Anglo-Norman origin): habitational name from Burgh (Castle) in Suffolk, England. Burk represents an Anglo-Norman pronunciation of Old English burg, Middle English burgh ‘fortification’ (see Burgh ).
German: variant of   Burk  .

variants.name: Burton
meanings: English: habitational name from a placename that is very common in central and northern England. The derivation in most cases is from Old English burh ‘fort’ (see Burke ) + tūn ‘enclosure, settlement’.
French (northeastern): regional variant of   Berton  .

variants.name: Burkett
meanings: English: from an Old English personal name, Burgheard, composed of the elements burh, burg ‘fort’ (see Burke ) + heard ‘hardy, brave, strong’. The name was reintroduced into Middle English by the Normans in the forms Bou(r)chart, Bocard.

English: possibly also from a Middle English personal name Burcard, Burchard (Norman Burcard, Old French Burchard), which was imported by the Normans and derives from ancient Germanic Burghard (in the form Burkhard this was a very popular medieval German name; see Burkhart ), cognate with the Old English name above. In France this gives rise to the surname Bouchard , Bouchart, but evidence for its use in Norman England is difficult to pin down and modern surnames in Burch- often have a different origin altogether.
English: variant of   Birkett  , a spelling commonly found in northern England.

variants.name: Bourke
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Dermot, Liam, Murphy.
Irish: variant of   Burke  .
Americanized form of French   Bourg   or of its altered form   Bourque  .

variants.name: Burgh
meanings: English and Scottish: habitational name from one or other of the ten English places named Burgh in northern England and East Anglia, or the six named Brough in Derbyshire, Nottinghamshire, Westmorland, and Yorkshire. All these are from Old English burg, burh, Middle English burgh ‘massive earthwork, fortification; manor, borough’. Compare Bury . The placename Burgh is pronounced as borough. The surname Brough arose equally from places now called Burgh and Brough, and is the more common form of the surname in modern Britain, where it is pronounced bruff or broof (/brʊf/). The Scottish surname was probably brought in from Yorkshire. For the surname in Ireland see Burke .

variants.name: Burkeen
meanings: American pet form of Irish   Burke  , not found as a surname in Ireland.
Possibly an altered form of Swiss German Bürgin (see Burgin ).

variants.name: Borke
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Fritz.
German (also Börke): from a pet form of a Slavic personal name containing the element bor ‘to fight’.
German (also Börke): topographic name for someone living near a pine forest, from Slavic bor ‘pine tree, fir tree, coniferous forest’.

variants.name: Duxbury
meanings: English (Lancashire and Yorkshire): habitational name from Duxbury in Lancashire, recorded in the early 13th century as D(e)ukesbiri, from the genitive case of the Old English personal name Deowuc or Duc(c) (both of uncertain origin) + Old English burh ‘fort’ (see Burke ).

variants.name: Borrowman
meanings: Scottish (Midlothian and Dumfriesshire): status name from Middle English burghman, borughman (Old English burhmann) ‘inhabitant of a (fortified) town’ (see Burke ), especially one holding land or buildings by burgage (see Burgess ).

variants.name: Burman
meanings: English: status name, from Middle English burghman, borughman (Old English burhmann) ‘inhabitant of a (fortified) town’ (see Burke ), especially one holding land or buildings by burgage (see Burgess ).
English: variant of   Bowerman  .
Americanized form of German Buhrmann or Bührmann.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

en.lifeSummary: When Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde was born in 1340, in Clanricarde, County Galway, Ireland, his father, Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde, was 25 and his mother, Grany O'Carroll, was 21. He married More O'Madden about 1363, in Ireland. They were the parents of at least 4 sons. He died in 1387, at the age of 47, and was buried in Athenry, County Galway, Ireland.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde est né en 1340, à Clanricarde, County Galway, Irlande, son père, Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde, avait 25 ans et sa mère, Grany O'Carroll, avait 21 ans. Il a épousé More O'Madden environ 1363, à Irlande. Ils ont eu au moins 4 fils. Il est décédé en 1387, à l’âge de 47 ans, et a été enterré à Athenry, County Galway, Irlande.

de.lifeSummary: Als Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde 1340 in Clanricarde, County Galway, Irland geboren wurde, war sein Vater Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde 25 und seine Mutter Grany O'Carroll 21. Er hat um 1363 in Irland More O'Madden geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 4 Söhne. Er starb 1387 im Alter von 47 Jahren und wurde in Athenry, County Galway, Irland begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde è nato il nel 1340 nel Clanricarde, County Galway, Irlanda, il suo padre, Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde, aveva 25 anni e il suo madre, Grany O'Carroll, aveva 21 anni. Lui sposato More O'Madden circa 1363, nel Irlanda. Erano genitori di almeno 4 figli. Morì nel 1387, all'età di 47 anni, e fu sepolto a Athenry, County Galway, Irlanda.

ja.Galway, County Galway, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ ガルワイガルワイ
ja.Dominican church of Saints Peter and Pau: アイルランドコウントイ ガルワイアスエンリー
ja.Clanricarde, Galway, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ ガルワイクランリカード
ja.Clanricarde, Galway, Connaught, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ ガルワイクランリカード
ja.lifeSummary: Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricardeが1340年にアイルランドコウントイ ガルワイクランリカードで生まれた時，父Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricardeは25歳，母Grany O'Carrollは21歳であった。約1363年にアイルランドでMore O'Maddenと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも4人の息子の親であった。彼は1387年に47歳で死去し，アイルランドコウントイ ガルワイアスエンリーにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde(이)가 1340년 아일랜드 카운티 갈웨이 클랜리카드에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde(은)는 25세였고, 그의 어머니 Grany O'Carroll(은)는 21세였다. 그는 대략 1363년 아일랜드에서 More O'Madden(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 네 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1387년 47세의 나이로 사망했으며 아일랜드 카운티 갈웨이 앳헨리에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde nasceu em 1340, em Clanricarde, County Galway, Irlanda, seu pai, Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde, tinha 25 anos e sua mãe, Grany O'Carroll, tinha 21 anos. Ele casou-se com More O'Madden aproximadamente 1363, em Irlanda. Eles tiveram pelo menos 4 filhos. Ele faleceu em 1387, com 47 anos, e foi sepultado em Athenry, County Galway, Irlanda.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde родился в 1340 г. в местности кланрикард, коунти галвеи, Ирландия, его отцу по имени Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde было 25 лет, а матери, которую звали Grany O'Carroll, 21. Он женился на женщине по имени More O'Madden около 1363 г. в местности Ирландия. У них было по крайней мере 4 сына. Он умер в 1387 г. в возрасте 47 лет и был похоронен в местности афенри, коунти галвеи, Ирландия.

sm.from 1353 to 1387: mai le 1353 e o'o I le 1387

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Richard Óg Burke 2nd of Clanricarde nació en 1340, en Clanricarde, County Galway, Irlanda, su papá, Uilleag de Burgh 1st of Clanricarde, tenía 25 años y su mamá, Grany O'Carroll, tenía 21 años. Se casó con More O'Madden aproximadamente 1363, en Irlanda. Fueron padres de por lo menos 4 hijos. Murió en 1387, a la edad de 47 años, y fue sepultado en Athenry, County Galway, Irlanda.
